1: The temple vision of Ezekiel 40-48 begins with a precise date formula: “the twenty-fifth year of our exile, at the start of the year, on the tenth of the month, fourteen years after the city had fallen.” Working from the first deportation of 597 BCE, the 25th year = 573/572 BCE. The “start of the year” (berosh hashana) and “tenth of the month” is the tenth of Tishri — Yom Kippur, the Day of Atonement, the holiest day of the Hebrew calendar and the one day when the high priest entered the Most Holy Place. Ezekiel’s vision of the new temple on Yom Kippur of the 25th year of exile (the mid-point of a jubilee cycle) is theologically deliberate. The 25th year is also the beginning of the second half of the 50-year jubilee cycle, when enslaved Israelites were to be freed and land returned (Lev 25).
2: “In divine visions he brought me to the land of Israel and set me on a very high mountain, on whose southern slope was something resembling a city.” The visionary transport to Israel (marot Elohim, divine visions) echoes the inaugural vision of Ezekiel 1 and the temple-departure vision of Ezekiel 8-11. The “very high mountain” is Ezekiel’s new Zion — the eschatological mountain that fulfills Isaiah 2:2 (“the mountain of the house of the LORD shall be established as the highest mountain”). The “something resembling a city” on the southern slope is the visionary temple complex seen from above — a city-sized sacred precinct that Ezekiel is about to explore in meticulous priestly detail.
3: “He brought me there, and I saw a man whose appearance was like gleaming bronze (mareh nechoshet), holding a linen measuring cord and a measuring reed, standing in the gateway.” The angelic guide of Ezekiel 40-48 is a divine messenger whose function is to measure and reveal the temple’s dimensions to Ezekiel. The “gleaming bronze” appearance connects to the angelic figure of Daniel 10:6 (whose body is like topaz and face like lightning) and Revelation 1:15 (the risen Christ whose feet are like refined bronze). The measuring tools — a linen cord for long distances and a measuring reed for shorter measurements — establish the architectural precision of what follows.
4: The angelic guide’s command — “look with your eyes, hear with your ears, and fix your attention on everything I am showing you — for you were brought here precisely so that I might show it to you” — is one of the most direct prophetic-commissioning speeches in the OT. The emphatic “precisely so that I might show it to you” signals that every measurement Ezekiel receives is intentional divine disclosure. The command “report all that you see to the house of Israel” makes Ezekiel a precise mediator: he is to transmit the architectural measurements with priestly accuracy, not metaphorical approximation.
5: “There was a wall surrounding the outside of the temple complex.” The outer boundary wall establishes the sacred precinct’s perimeter. The “long cubit” (amah vetefach, a standard cubit plus a handbreadth) is the unit of measurement throughout Ezekiel 40-48 — approximately 20.4 inches (52 cm), slightly longer than the ordinary cubit of 17.5 inches (44.5 cm). The outer wall is one measuring rod (six long cubits) thick and one rod high — establishing a massive, imposing boundary that clearly separates the sacred from the common. Ezekiel’s priestly background makes the precise specification of the boundary wall theologically significant: the wall is what makes holiness possible by defining sacred space.
6: “He came to the east-facing gate, climbed its steps, and measured the threshold of the gate: one reed deep.” The east gate is the first and primary gate measured — because east is the direction of the divine glory’s approach (Ezekiel 43:2: “the glory of God came from the east”). The steps up to the gate threshold establish the progressive ascent of holiness: the outer court is higher than the surrounding land, the inner court higher than the outer (Ezekiel 40:22: seven steps to the outer gate; Ezekiel 40:31: eight steps to the inner). The measurement sequence — outer east gate (Ezekiel 40:6-16), outer court (Ezekiel 40:17-19), outer north gate (Ezekiel 40:20-23), outer south gate (Ezekiel 40:24-27), inner south gate (Ezekiel 40:28-31), inner east gate (Ezekiel 40:32-34), inner north gate (Ezekiel 40:35-37) — follows a complete circuit of the temple complex.
7: “The guardrooms were each one reed long and one reed wide; the spaces between the guardrooms were five cubits.” Each of the outer gates contains six guardrooms (tasot) — three on each side of the gatehouse passageway. The six-chambered gatehouse structure is attested archaeologically at Bronze Age and Iron Age Israelite sites including Megiddo, Hazor, Gezer, and (though smaller) Solomon’s gate at Lachish — Yigael Yadin’s 1958 discovery showed these sites share the same six-chambered plan, which he connected to Solomon’s building program (1 Kings 9:15). The guardrooms provided security posts for controlling access to the sacred precinct.
8: “He measured the inner porch of the gate: one reed.” The systematic measurement of each component of the gate complex — threshold (Ezekiel 40:6), guardrooms (Ezekiel 40:7), inner porch (Ezekiel 40:8), entry porch (Ezekiel 40:9), outer wall (Ezekiel 40:5) — reflects priestly precision in defining the sacred architecture. Each gate element has its assigned dimension; nothing is approximate or provisional. The consistent precision across all gates (outer east, outer north, outer south, inner south, inner east, inner north) creates a temple of exact symmetry and order, reflecting the divine character of its designer and the ordered holiness of its occupant.
9: “He measured the gate’s entry porch: eight cubits; its doorposts: two cubits. The entry porch faced inward.” The entry porch (ulam hasha-ar) is the vestibule that opens toward the interior of the sacred precinct — “faced inward” (ulam hasha-ar mibayit) establishes the orientation of worshippers as they enter: the gatehouse draws them inward toward the presence of YHWH rather than allowing a view through to the outside. The doorposts (elim) at two cubits width regulate the actual opening through which worshippers pass, establishing the physical boundary of transition into holy space.
10: “The guardrooms of the east gate — three on one side, three on the other — were all the same size; the doorposts on either side matched as well.” The uniformity of the guardrooms — identical dimensions on both sides, matching doorposts — creates a structure of perfect bilateral symmetry. In ancient Near Eastern architecture, architectural symmetry expressed cosmic order; in the OT, the tabernacle and temple were also characterized by exact symmetrical arrangements (Exod 26: the two sides of the tabernacle are mirror images). The uniform guardrooms signal that the approach to YHWH’s presence is consistently ordered — not arbitrary or varying.
11: “He measured the width of the gate opening: ten cubits, and the gate passage’s total length: thirteen cubits.” The gate opening of ten cubits is wide enough for processions but controlled enough to maintain sacred boundary integrity. The total passage length of thirteen cubits includes the gatehouse interior. These dimensions have been the subject of extensive architectural reconstruction; the most careful analyses (by scholars such as Moshe Greenberg and Daniel Block) place the gatehouse measurements within the parameters of known ancient Near Eastern monumental architecture, though the Ezekielian design is distinctively larger and more elaborate than any known historical temple gate.
12: “A low barrier of one cubit stood in front of each guardroom on either side, and each guardroom measured six cubits square.” The low barrier (gvul, one cubit high) in front of each guardroom is a short partition wall that defined the guardroom space without fully closing it off. The six-cubit-square guardroom dimensions create a modular unit that is repeated with perfect consistency throughout the gate complex. The consistent use of modular measurement suggests a design principle of sacred geometry: the entire temple complex is built from repeating proportional units that express mathematical order as an attribute of divine holiness.
13: “He measured the full width of the gate, ceiling to ceiling across the opposite guardrooms: twenty-five cubits, door facing door.” The total width of twenty-five cubits (from outside wall to outside wall of the gatehouse) confirms the bilateral symmetry: the gatehouse is exactly 25 cubits wide at its widest point. This measurement (25 cubits) recurs throughout the temple complex and becomes, in ch. Ezekiel 48:15-18, the dimension of the city’s pastureland strips — a sacred proportional unit that ties the architectural and territorial dimensions of the eschatological community together.
14: “He made the doorposts sixty cubits high, and the court ran around the gate on every side.” The sixty-cubit height of the doorposts creates a monumental gateway that would have been visible from a great distance — comparable to the monumental gateways of Egyptian temples (the First Pylon of Karnak was approximately 43 meters tall). The visual dominance of the gate emphasizes the primacy of access and approach: the temple complex is first a place of entry before it is a place of sacrifice. The court surrounding the gate integrates the gateway into the broader architectural complex.
15: “From the outer face of the gate entrance to the inner face of the gate’s vestibule: fifty cubits.” The total depth of fifty cubits for each outer gate complex is the governing measurement of the gate structures. Fifty cubits is the jubilee number (Leviticus 25:10: the 50th year is the jubilee year of release), and its consistent use as the gate depth throughout the temple complex may be theologically deliberate — the entrance to YHWH’s presence is structured around the jubilee measurement, connecting access to the temple with the jubilee themes of freedom, restoration, and return.
16: “There were recessed windows facing inward in the guardrooms and their doorposts, and in the vestibules as well — windows all around the interior. Palm trees adorned each doorpost.” The “recessed windows” (chaloni shekufim atumim) are the same term used for Solomon’s temple windows (1 Kings 6:4) — narrowly splayed windows that let in light while limiting outward view, directing the worshipper’s attention inward. The palm trees (timorim) decorating the doorposts anticipate the elaborate palm-tree motif of the inner temple (Ezekiel 41:18-20) — the palm is a symbol of the righteous (Psalms 92:12) and of the paradisal garden (Song of Solomon 7:7-8), connecting the temple with Eden-imagery throughout Ezekiel 40-48.
17: “He brought me into the outer court; I saw chambers arranged all around and a pavement running the circuit, with thirty chambers fronting the pavement.” The outer court (chatzer hachitsonah) is the large open space between the outer gates and the inner gates. Thirty chambers (lishkot) line the outer court — these are likely storage and preparation rooms for worshippers and their offerings. The “pavement” (ritzpah) is a paved walkway connecting the outer gates and fronting all thirty chambers — an impressive architectural feature providing covered access around the entire outer court.
18: “The pavement ran alongside the gates, matching their length; this was called the lower pavement.” The designation “lower pavement” (ritzpah hataschtona) implies a corresponding “upper pavement” in the inner court, creating a vertical hierarchy of sacred space: outer court (lower) and inner court (higher). The progressive ascent from lower to higher as one moves toward YHWH’s presence is both architectural and theological — each step toward the divine presence is a step upward, reflecting the biblical metaphor of “going up” to worship YHWH in Jerusalem (the pilgrim psalms are “songs of ascent”).
19: “He measured the distance from the front of the lower outer gate to the exterior of the inner court: a hundred cubits on the east and a hundred cubits on the north.” The consistent hundred-cubit spacing between the outer gates and the inner gates is the governing measurement of the outer court. This creates a vast open plaza between the two gate systems — approximately 175 feet (53 meters) of open space that could accommodate large numbers of worshippers. The regularity (exactly 100 cubits on both east and north sides, presumably also south) confirms the perfect square proportions of the temple complex.
20: “He then measured the length and width of the north-facing gate of the outer court.” The systematic measurement of the outer north gate begins the second gate complex in the circuit. The north gate is described with the same measurements as the east gate (Ezekiel 40:21-23 confirm all dimensions match) — establishing that all three outer gates (east, north, south) are structurally identical. The uniformity of the gates means that access to the outer court is equally available from three directions; the inner court likewise has three gates (south, east, north) arranged to align directly with the outer gates.
21: “The guardrooms — three on each side — and its doorposts and vestibule matched the first gate’s dimensions: fifty cubits long, twenty-five cubits wide.” The explicit confirmation that the north gate matches the east gate initiates the repetitive formula that governs all subsequent gate descriptions in Ezekiel 40. The repetition is not literary carelessness but architectural precision: in a priestly document, consistency in measurement signals that the design is not approximate but exact. The scholar who studies these chapters must attend to the small variations (Ezekiel 40:22: seven steps for outer gates; Ezekiel 40:31: eight steps for inner gates) because the differences are architecturally meaningful.
22: “Its windows, vestibule, and palm trees matched the dimensions of the east gate; seven steps led up to it, with the vestibule in front.” The seven steps to the outer gates (mentioned here for the outer north gate) establish the minimum elevation change between the surrounding terrain and the outer court level. Seven steps connecting the ground level to the outer court sets the base level of the sacred complex above the ordinary world. The palm trees on the vestibule doorposts appear here for the outer north gate, confirming that all outer gates have palm-tree decoration — a consistent Eden/paradisal motif throughout the complex.
23: “The inner court had gates directly opposite the north and east outer gates; the measured distance from outer gate to inner gate was a hundred cubits.” The alignment of inner and outer gates — inner gates directly facing outer gates across the hundred-cubit outer court — creates three processional axes (north, east, and south) through the entire complex. A worshipper entering the outer east gate could see directly through the outer court to the inner east gate and, ultimately, to the temple entrance. The visual alignment of gates and temple creates a sacred axis along which the eye (and the worshipper) moves from the outside world toward the divine presence.
24: “He led me southward, where I saw a south gate.” The southern outer gate completes the three-gate outer court system. The circuit of measurement — east, then north, then south — encompasses three of the four compass directions. The absence of a west outer gate is significant: the temple building itself is located on the west side of the inner court, meaning the western boundary of the inner court is the outer wall of the temple. No western access is needed because the western side is occupied by the sanctuary itself.
25: “It and its vestibule had windows all around, like the other gates, and measured fifty cubits long by twenty-five cubits wide.” The consistent fifty-by-twenty-five cubit measurements for the outer south gate confirm the standard gate template. The phrase “like the other gates” (kemidot hasha-arim harishonim) explicitly connects this gate to the previous measurements, reinforcing that the architectural uniformity is intentional. The priestly emphasis on exact consistency throughout the temple design is not merely aesthetic but theological: YHWH’s house is not built to human whim but to divine specification.
26: “Seven steps led up to it, the vestibule facing them; palm trees adorned its doorposts, one on each side.” The identical seven-step approach, inward-facing vestibule, and palm-tree doorposts confirm that all three outer gates share a single design template. The seven steps (for outer gates) vs. eight steps (for inner gates) creates a graduated approach: each transition to higher holiness requires an additional step. Seven (outer) and eight (inner) — the outer corresponds to the creation week’s sabbath completion; the inner’s eight steps correspond to the eighth day, the new-creation day that goes beyond the sabbatical cycle.
27: “There was a south-facing gate in the inner court; he measured a hundred cubits from outer gate to inner gate on the south side.” The inner south gate begins the second set of three gate measurements (inner south, inner east, inner north). The hundred-cubit spacing confirmed on the south side establishes the complete symmetry of the outer court: all three axes (north, east, south) have exactly one hundred cubits from outer gate to inner gate. The inner court’s three gates are the penultimate boundary before the sacred courts immediately surrounding the temple building itself.
28: “He brought me through the south gate into the inner court and measured the south gate; its measurements were the same as all the others.” The transition from outer to inner court is marked by being “brought through” rather than merely being led alongside — Ezekiel’s entry into the inner court signals a deepening of the vision. The inner south gate has the same measurements as the outer gates (fifty cubits by twenty-five cubits) — the gate template is consistent across both the outer and inner perimeters, though the eight-step approach (Ezekiel 40:31) distinguishes the inner gates from the outer.
29: “Its guardrooms, doorposts, and vestibule matched the standard dimensions; it and its vestibule had windows all around — fifty cubits long, twenty-five cubits wide.” The repetition of the standard dimensions for the inner south gate confirms that the inner gates are architecturally identical to the outer gates in their structural elements. The windows “all around” (saviv saviv chaloni) fill the inner gate with light — the windows are an inward-facing design feature that floods the sacred passageway with natural light as one enters. The architectural language of light permeating the sacred space anticipates the glory-filled temple of Ezekiel 43.
30: “The vestibules around the gates were twenty-five cubits long and five cubits wide.” This verse specifies the vestibule dimensions for the inner gates — twenty-five cubits long and five cubits wide — creating a proportionally narrower vestibule than the gate passage itself. The vestibule functions as a transitional space: a boundary zone between the open court and the controlled gate passageway. In sacred architecture, vestibules serve as decompression spaces where the transition between profane and sacred is experienced architecturally before the formal threshold crossing.
31: “Its vestibule faced the outer court, with palm trees adorning its doorposts; its stairway had eight steps.” The inner gate vestibule faces the outer court (rather than inward) — a deliberate reversal from the outer gate vestibule, which faced inward (Ezekiel 40:9). This architectural reversal means that each gate complex is oriented toward the court it controls access to: outer gates face inward toward the outer court; inner gates face outward toward the outer court. The eight steps to the inner gates (vs. seven to the outer) are the most structurally significant distinction: the inner courts are at higher elevation, requiring an additional step, marking the transition to a holier level of sacred space.
32: “He brought me to the east side of the inner court and measured the east inner gate; it matched the standard dimensions.” The inner east gate — the most theologically significant inner gate, because it aligns with the divine glory’s entry from the east (Ezekiel 43:2-4) — is measured and confirmed as structurally identical to the inner south gate. The inner east gate is the gate through which the divine glory re-enters the temple (Ezekiel 43:4), making it the sacred axis of the entire vision: outer east gate → inner east gate → temple vestibule → sanctuary → Most Holy Place is the processional line of divine approach.
33: “Its guardrooms, doorposts, and vestibule matched the same standard dimensions; it and its vestibule had windows all around — fifty cubits long, twenty-five cubits wide.” The consistent repetition of the standard dimensions for the inner east gate confirms the template. By this point in the chapter, Ezekiel’s readers have received the full dimensions for six gates (three outer, three inner) — all identical in structural layout, differing only in approach steps (seven vs. eight). The repetitive format, while potentially tedious for modern readers, functions for its original audience as the equivalent of an architectural blueprint being read aloud: each gate’s measurement is confirmed before moving on.
34: “Its vestibule faced the outer court, with palm trees on its doorposts on each side; its stairway had eight steps.” The inner east gate’s vestibule faces the outer court (like all inner gates), and its eight-step approach is confirmed. The palm trees on the inner gate doorposts appear consistently throughout the inner gate complex, connecting the inner courts to the Eden-imagery that becomes explicit in the Most Holy Place’s architectural program (Ezekiel 41:18-26: cherubim and palm trees alternating on every wall). The inner courts are architecturally moving the worshipper toward an Eden-like space, the dwelling of the divine presence.
35: “He brought me to the north inner gate and measured it; it was the same as all the others.” The inner north gate — the third and final inner gate — completes the circuit of the inner court. The measurement circuit (outer east → outer north → outer south → inner south → inner east → inner north) follows a specific directional pattern: east first (most sacred), then counter-clockwise for the outer courts, then south-first for the inner courts. The directional pattern may reflect liturgical procession patterns known from Israel’s worship traditions, or the specific requirements of Ezekiel’s visionary experience.
36: “Its guardrooms, doorposts, and vestibule all measured the same; windows ran around all sides — fifty cubits long and twenty-five cubits wide.” The inner north gate’s identical dimensions complete the six-gate survey. The windowsall-around motif — present in every inner gate — creates an interior environment flooded with diffuse light from all directions. In a pre-electric architectural context, windows “all around” would produce a remarkably luminous interior, appropriate for a space through which worshippers transition toward the divine presence whose characteristic biblical manifestation is light and fire.
37: “Its vestibule faced the outer court, with palm trees on its doorposts on each side; its stairway had eight steps.” The three features — outward-facing vestibule, palm-tree doorposts, eight steps — are confirmed for the inner north gate, completing the inner gate template. By this point in the vision, the structural repetition has achieved its purpose: the reader has internalized the template of the six-chamber gatehouse with inward vestibule (outer gates), outward vestibule (inner gates), windows all around, and palm-tree decoration. The detail-orientation of the vision insists on the complete architectural knowledge of YHWH’s house as a prerequisite for understanding its significance.
38: “A chamber with its doorway was built beside the inner gate’s doorposts, where the burnt offering was washed before presentation.” This slaughter and washing chamber beside the inner gate is the first functional space mentioned in the tour — all previous measurements were structural. The washing of the burnt offering before presentation connects to the ritual purification requirements of Leviticus 1:9 (the entrails and legs of the burnt offering were washed before burning). The placement of the washing chamber beside the inner gate’s doorposts establishes that ritual preparation for the holiest offerings occurred at the boundary between outer and inner courts.
39: “In the gate’s vestibule, two tables stood on each side — four in total — on which the burnt offering, sin offering, and guilt offering were slaughtered.” The four tables in the inner gate vestibule are slaughter-tables for the three major categories of Levitical sacrifice: burnt offering (olah), sin offering (chatat), and guilt offering (asham). The placement of the slaughter tables in the vestibule — the transitional space between outer and inner courts — is architecturally significant: the sacrificial slaughter occurs at the boundary, establishing that approaching YHWH requires the prior mediation of blood sacrifice. The four tables (two on each side) are positioned symmetrically, matching the bilateral symmetry of the entire gate complex.
40: “On the outside, beside the porch at the entrance of the north gate, two tables stood on each side of the gate porch.” The four additional tables at the north gate porch exterior bring the total to eight tables in or around the inner north gate area (Ezekiel 40:39-43). The north gate is apparently the primary entrance for the sacrificial system — the gate through which sacrificial animals were brought in. The eight tables (four inside the vestibule, four outside) provide ample surface area for the volume of slaughter required for the temple’s sacrificial operation. The outer tables (at the porch exterior) served as the initial reception point for animals brought in.
41: “Four tables on each side of the gate — eight tables in total — on which the sacrificial animals were slaughtered.” The summary statement confirms the total of eight slaughter tables associated with the inner north gate. The concentration of slaughter infrastructure at the north gate reflects the OT’s sacrificial architecture: in the Mosaic tabernacle and the Jerusalem temple, the northern side of the altar was used for slaughter (Leviticus 1:11: “he shall kill it on the north side of the altar before YHWH”). The north-side slaughter convention is institutionalized in the new temple’s architectural design.
42: “The four tables for the burnt offering were of cut stone (gazit), each one and a half cubits long, a cubit and a half wide, and one cubit high. On them were laid the implements used for slaughtering the burnt offering and the sacrifice.” The stone tables contrast with the wooden altar table of Ezekiel 41:22 (the altar of wood before YHWH): stone tables are for preparation (cutting, washing); the wooden altar is for the sacred offering. The precise dimensions of the stone tables — 1.5 × 1.5 × 1 cubit — create a sturdy, low surface appropriate for the work of priestly slaughter. The listing of implements on the tables grounds the priestly vision in the practical liturgy of Levitical worship.
43: “Double hooks, a handbreadth long, were fastened all around the inside walls, and the flesh of the offerings rested on the tables.” The metal hooks (shedayyim, double hooks or pegs) fastened to the interior walls served to hang portions of the sacrifice during the preparation process. This detail has parallels in archaeological remains of ancient cultic sites — at Tel Megiddo and Tel Beer-sheba, cultic installations include evidence of hooks or pegs in cultic contexts. The “flesh of the offerings” resting on the tables identifies these as work surfaces for priestly butchery, a practical architectural feature that grounds the vision in the physical reality of sacrificial worship.
44: “Outside the inner gate in the inner court were chambers for the singers: one beside the north inner gate facing south, and one beside the south inner gate facing north.” The chambers for the singers (lishkot hashirim) adjacent to the inner gates introduce the Levitical music ministry into the temple complex. The temple singers (established by David in 1 Chr 25 and confirmed by Solomon in 2 Chronicles 5:12-14) occupied specific positions in the temple precincts. The placement of singers’ chambers adjacent to the inner gates means that the transition from outer to inner court was accompanied by sacred music — the worshipper entered the heightened sacred space to the sound of the Levitical choir.
45: “He said to me: This south-facing chamber is for the priests who are responsible for the care of the temple.” The angelic guide breaks from pure measurement to provide a functional explanation — the chambers have designated occupants. The priests responsible for the temple’s care (shmirat habayt) are the temple-maintenance staff: priests assigned to the daily round of lighting the lamps, maintaining the incense, and keeping the sacred spaces in order. Their chamber’s south-facing orientation (toward the temple building) reflects their role as caretakers always oriented toward the sanctuary they serve.
46: “The north-facing chamber is for the priests who have charge of the altar. These are the sons of Zadok — those Levites who draw near to the LORD to serve him.” The bene Tzadoq (sons of Zadok) are the Zadokite priests who alone are authorized to approach YHWH’s altar in the new temple (cf. Ezekiel 44:15-16). Zadok was the priest loyal to David and Solomon (1 Kings 1:8; Ezekiel 2:35), whose lineage alone continued after Solomon’s temple was established. Ezekiel’s elevation of the Zadokites over the general Levitical clergy (Ezekiel 44:10-16) is a priestly reform embedded in the visionary architecture — the new temple’s altar service is restricted to the faithful priestly line.
47: “He measured the inner court: a hundred cubits long and a hundred cubits wide — a perfect square. The altar stood in front of the temple.” The inner court is a perfect square (100 × 100 cubits) — the ideal form in ancient sacred architecture representing cosmic order and completeness. The outer court was also a square (cf. Ezekiel 40:19: 100 cubits on east and north sides), creating a nested set of perfect squares. “The altar stood in front of the temple” — the bronze altar of burnt offering occupies the center of the inner court, directly before the temple entrance, establishing the sacrificial altar as the spatial center of the inner court’s liturgical program.
48: “He brought me to the temple vestibule and measured its doorposts: five cubits on each side. The width of the gate passage was three cubits on each side.” The temple vestibule (ulam habayit) is the outer porch of the temple building itself — the threshold between the inner court and the temple’s interior. The vestibule doorposts at five cubits on each side create a substantial frame. The “gate passage” width of three cubits on each side (total six cubits for the entrance opening) is considerably narrower than the outer and inner gate passages — the approach to the divine presence narrows as holiness increases, culminating in the small, dark Most Holy Place at the temple’s innermost chamber.
49: “The vestibule was twenty cubits long and eleven cubits wide; steps led up to it, and columns flanked the doorposts on either side.” The temple vestibule’s dimensions — twenty cubits long, eleven cubits wide — are slightly larger than Solomon’s temple vestibule (1 Kings 6:3: twenty cubits long, ten cubits wide), suggesting that Ezekiel’s temple, while sharing the basic proportions of Solomon’s, is deliberately grander. The columns flanking the doorposts echo Jachin and Boaz (1 Kings 7:15-22), the two free-standing columns at Solomon’s temple entrance — architectural features associated with the royal Davidic temple and its divine patronage. Chapter 40 ends at the temple threshold: Ezekiel 41 will measure the temple’s interior.
